steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a choice that recounts the sequence of events the researchers proposed. That means the answer must include all three stages — in order: Chrysalis approached Saturn roughly 160 million years ago, tidal forces broke it apart, and the debris became Saturn’s rings.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — According to researchers' simulations, debris from Saturn's rings would likely have caused Chrysalis—a large moon orbiting Saturn—to break apart around 160 million years ago.: reverses cause and effect — the debris formed the rings; the rings did not cause the breakup. ✗
  • Choice B — In a 2022 study, researchers proposed that Saturn was once orbited by a large moon that broke apart.: omits the 160-million-year timing and the formation of the rings. Not a full sequence. ✗
  • Choice C — Around 160 million years ago, a large moon (Chrysalis) may have come close enough to Saturn that tidal forces broke the moon apart; its debris then formed the planet's rings.: covers all three steps in the correct order — timing, breakup by tidal forces, rings formed from debris. ✓
  • Choice D — If a moon orbiting a planet (like Saturn) comes close enough to that planet, tidal forces can cause the moon to break apart.: states only the general principle from note 1; never mentions Chrysalis, the timing, or the rings. ✗
C is correct.
